agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A new 'auto-split' feature is proposed that would automatically divide shipments exceeding 500 kg into multiple sub-shipments, each assigned to a different carrier, bypassing supervisor involvement. This directly conflicts with two established production invariants in the existing domain model.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods to be transported, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The entity responsible for physically transporting a Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept — a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment that exceeds a weight threshold. Does NOT exist in the current domain model."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment","definition":"The binding of exactly one Carrier to a Shipment. Once set, this is locked and can only be changed by a Supervisor."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role that is the sole authority permitted to reassign a Carrier on a Shipment."},{"term":"Auto-split","definition":"A proposed automatic process that divides an overweight Shipment into multiple sub-shipments and assigns each to a different Carrier without Supervisor approval. This concept contradicts the existing assignment invariant and Supervisor authority rule."},{"term":"Dispatch","definition":"The point at which a Shipment is sent out for delivery. The system guarantees every Shipment has exactly one Carrier at this point."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint. A Shipment without exactly one Carrier is in a corrupt state — accountability, tracking, liability, and dispatch all depend on this 1:1 relationship. If violated, the system cannot determine who is responsible for the goods.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in any non-terminal state, COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an access-control integrity constraint protecting the chain of custody. Allowing unsupervised reassignment breaks the audit trail and accountability model. The Supervisor gate exists to prevent unauthorized or erroneous reassignment of responsibility for goods in transit.","testable_assertion":"Any state transition that changes the Carrier on a Shipment must be initiated by an actor with the Supervisor role; otherwise, the transition is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier (dispatch invariant).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Physical constraint — a shipment leaves on exactly one vehicle with one responsible party. Without this, goods can be lost, duplicated, or unaccounted for.","testable_assertion":"The dispatch operation must fail if the Shipment does not have exactly one assigned Carrier."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A 501 kg shipment is auto-split into two sub-shipments, each assigned to a different carrier. The original shipment now has zero carriers or two carriers.","expected_behavior":"This violates BR-1 (exactly one carrier per shipment). The domain model has no concept of a parent shipment delegating its carrier assignment to children. The system state becomes corrupt."},{"scenario":"Auto-split reassigns carrier responsibility without supervisor involvement.","expected_behavior":"This violates BR-2. The feature explicitly bypasses the supervisor gate, which is an invariant protecting chain-of-custody integrity."},{"scenario":"A shipment is split after a carrier has already been assigned.","expected_behavior":"The original carrier assignment is implicitly revoked or duplicated. Neither outcome is permitted without supervisor action under the current model."},{"scenario":"One sub-shipment's carrier fails delivery; who owns the liability for the original shipment?","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The current model assumes one carrier = one accountable party. Splitting breaks this accountability chain with no replacement model defined."},{"scenario":"Weight is exactly 500 kg.","expected_behavior":"Ambiguous — the spec says 'exceeds 500 kg' so 500 kg exactly should NOT trigger a split, but this boundary needs explicit clarification."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk. Splitting shipments across multiple carriers changes the cost structure (multiple pickups, multiple deliveries, multiple contracts). Liability allocation for damaged/lost goods in a split shipment is undefined. Insurance and claims processes assume a single carrier per shipment.","operational":"Severe. The auto-split feature introduces a new entity (sub-shipment) and a new relationship (parent-child shipments) that the current domain model does not support. Every downstream system — tracking, billing, dispatch, proof of delivery, returns — assumes one shipment = one carrier. Retrofitting all of these is a major operational change.","user":"Customers currently track one shipment with one carrier. Splitting creates confusion: multiple tracking numbers, multiple delivery windows, partial deliveries. The customer experience degrades unless a new aggregation/tracking model is built.","risk":"CRITICAL. This feature, as specified, violates two hard invariants. Shipping it as described would corrupt the domain model. Additionally, bypassing supervisor authority creates compliance and audit trail risks. If a split assignment goes wrong, there is no human checkpoint to catch errors.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Must handle multi-carrier coordination for what was previously a single-carrier workflow. Dispatch logic must be rewritten."},{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their authority over carrier assignment is bypassed. This undermines the control model they rely on for accountability."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"Now responsible for partial shipments. SLAs, contracts, and liability terms may not cover partial loads."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Receive partial deliveries from multiple carriers. Tracking and support experience degrades."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Cost allocation, invoicing, and insurance claims must handle split shipments — a model that does not exist today."},{"group":"Compliance / Legal","impact":"Chain of custody audit trail is broken when supervisor gate is bypassed. Regulatory implications for high-value or regulated goods."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction with two established production invariants (one-carrier-per-shipment and supervisor-only reassignment). Until the product team resolves whether to evolve the domain model or redesign the feature to work within existing invariants, no acceptance criteria can be confidently defined. The confidence is below 50 because the contradictions are fundamental, not edge-case.","high_confidence_areas":["The two invariants (BR-1 and BR-2) are clearly stated and in production","The proposed feature violates both invariants as currently specified","The feature introduces a sub-shipment concept that does not exist in the domain model"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends to formally evolve the domain model to support multi-carrier shipments","Whether 'sub-shipment' should be a first-class entity or a different aggregate entirely","What the liability and accountability model looks like for split shipments","Whether the 500 kg threshold is a hard rule or configurable policy"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model and its invariants are intentional and battle-tested in production","The product team may not have been aware of the invariant conflicts when writing this request"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature directly contradicts BR-1 (one carrier per shipment) and BR-2 (supervisor-only reassignment). A shipment split across multiple carriers violates the fundamental 1:1 carrier assignment invariant. Assigning carriers to sub-shipments without supervisor involvement violates the supervisor authority invariant. These are not edge cases — the feature as specified is structurally incompatible with the existing domain model.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Supervisors","Carriers","Customers","Finance","Compliance"],"options":["A: Reject the feature as specified. The domain model does not support multi-carrier shipments.","B: Evolve the domain model to introduce a 'Composite Shipment' aggregate that contains sub-shipments, each preserving the one-carrier invariant. The composite shipment itself is not dispatched — only its children are. This requires a formal domain model evolution with new aggregates, events, and invariants.","C: Keep the existing model but implement a 'pre-split' at order intake — before carrier assignment — so that each resulting shipment is a normal, independent shipment with its own single carrier. No parent-child relationship. The 'split' happens before the invariants apply.","D: Allow auto-split but require supervisor approval for the resulting carrier assignments, preserving BR-2 while relaxing BR-1 via a new Composite Shipment concept."],"recommendation":"Option C or D. Option C is the least disruptive: split overweight orders into independent shipments before carrier assignment, so each shipment honors all existing invariants. No new aggregates needed. Option D is appropriate if the business needs to maintain traceability between the original order and its parts, but requires domain model evolution. Option A (rejection) should be the default if the product team cannot clarify the intent. Option B is the most ambitious and should only be pursued if composite shipments are a long-term strategic need."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to what is logically one shipment","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier"},{"violation":"Auto-split bypasses supervisor authority for carrier assignment","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a Shipment"},{"violation":"Introduces 'sub-shipment' concept with no domain model support","business_rule_broken":"No aggregate, entity, or value object exists to represent a parent-child shipment relationship. The domain model has no place to put this."}]}
